channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: Witkoff and Kushner to Travel to Pakistan for Talks on Iran
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: The 85-Year-Old Widow Snagged by Trump’s Immigration Crackdown
item.description: In her first interview since being deported, Marie-Thérèse Ross-Mahé, the French widow of a former G.I., recounted her experience in ICE detention.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Porter
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times

item.title: Japan’s New Care Workers: Bodybuilders, Wrestlers and M.M.A. Fighters
item.description: Young athletes are being recruited to plug a staff shortage in Japan’s nursing homes. The results are reshaping elder care.
item.dc:creator: Chang W. Lee, Javier C. Hernández and Hisako Ueno
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times

item.title: Gaza Set to Hold First Local Election in Two Decades
item.description: Hamas said it was not participating in the municipal elections in Deir al-Balah, set for this weekend. Residents said it was a long-awaited opportunity to address the city’s problems
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon, Bilal Shbair, Abu Bakr Bashir, Iyad Abuheweila and Saher Alghorra
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times

item.title: Europeans Ask: What Would It Mean to Defend One Another Without NATO?
item.description: European Union nations have a little-known obligation to protect one another. Experts caution it is no replacement for NATO.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger and Jeanna Smialek
item.media:description: A meeting of NATO defense ministers at the group’s headquarters in Brussels in February. Europe is reconsidering its options for collective defense, as President Trump continues to threaten to leave the NATO alliance.

item.title: In Britain, 7 Unelected Lords Help Block an Assisted Dying Bill
item.description: A small group in the House of Lords proposed hundreds of amendments that helped stymie a bill that was meant to legalize medically assisted death for the terminally ill.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: Members of the House of Lords and House of Commons during the State Opening of Parliament in the House of Lords in London in 2023.

item.title: ‘Didi vs. Modi’: A Test for the Hindu Right in India’s Bengali Heartland
item.description: The party of Prime Minister Narendra Modi has been making gains in a state once beyond its grasp, but many decry an audit that removed nine million voters, many of them Muslim.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das, Hari Kumar and Atul Loke
item.media:description: Campaign posters featuring Prime Minister Narendra Modi of India, and Mamata Banerjee, the chief minister of West Bengal, who is also known by her moniker “Didi,” in Kolkata this month.

item.title: Netanyahu Reveals He Had Prostate Cancer and Underwent Radiation Therapy
item.description: The Israeli leader had surgery in 2024 for an enlarged prostate. More recently, follow-up care detected a tumor, his office disclosed on Friday.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger
item.media:credit: Avishag Shaar-Yashuv for The New York Times
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel delivering a speech in January. He said on Friday that he had delayed telling Israelis about his cancer for “two months.”

item.title: Britain and Spain Reject Reported Plans by Trump to Punish Them
item.description: An internal Pentagon email, reported by the Reuters news agency, suggested Washington was reviewing options to penalize the two nations for insufficiently supporting the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle and Emma Bubola
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: Prime Minister Keir Starmer of Britain in London on Wednesday. An internal Pentagon email suggested that America could withdraw support for Britain’s sovereignty over the Falkland Islands.

item.title: As Putin Orders That the Economy Be Fixed, Russia Grasps for Solutions
item.description: Another interest-rate cut highlights the narrowing path for the country’s central bank amid the strains of immense wartime spending.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko
item.media:credit: Nanna Heitmann for The New York Times
item.media:description: Stores in St. Petersburg, Russia, in October. The Kremlin has been struggling to find ways to revive the country’s lackluster economy.

item.title: Turkey Passes Legislation to Bar Children Under 15 From Social Media
item.description: The government says the measure, which must be signed into law by the president, will protect minors. Critics worry it will threaten free speech and privacy online.
item.dc:creator: Ben Hubbard and Safak Timur
item.media:description: Istanbul on Thursday. The government says the new legislation will protect children from virtual dangers including social media addiction, cyberbullying and commercial exploitation.

item.title: The Generals Who Are Now Running Iran
item.description: The killing of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei ushered in a new form of collective leadership in the country, with more power for the Revolutionary Guards.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Iranians gathered last month in Tehran with flags and posters to show their support for Mojtaba Khamenei, the new supreme leader.

item.title: $106 Billion Loan Reflects E.U.’s View That Peace in Ukraine Is Far Away
item.description: Unlike previous European assistance packages, this one is heavily weighted toward military spending, meant to put Ukraine on solid footing for a long fight.
item.dc:creator: Constant Méheut
item.media:credit: Mauricio Lima for The New York Times
item.media:description: A funeral for victims of a Russian strike in Ternopil, Ukraine, in November.

item.title: Touring Africa, Pope Leo Raised His Voice, but Didn’t Like the Echo
item.description: On his recent trip abroad, Leo XIV made some of his most forthright comments since becoming pope last year, but grew uncomfortable at how that criticism was interpreted.
item.dc:creator: Motoko Rich
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Pope Leo XIV in Bata, Equatorial Guinea, on Wednesday.

item.title: Rubio’s Absence From Iran Talks Highlights Stay-at-Home Role
item.description: President Trump has outsourced much of his diplomacy to others, while Mr. Rubio focuses on his second job as national security adviser.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: As President Trump prepares to send a delegation to the latest round of U.S.-Iran talks in Pakistan, his secretary of state, Marco Rubio, will remain where he often does: at home.

item.title: Kenyan Court Strikes Down Ruling Protecting Right to Abortion
item.description: The decision, in a country where thousands of women die yearly from unsafe abortions, held that abortions deprive unborn children of the “right to life.”
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.media:description: Activists protesting in Nairobi in 2019.

item.title: Drivers Stranded Overnight on Alberta, Canada, Highways After Snowstorm
item.description: The Royal Canadian Mounted Police said they were working to rescue those stranded and deliver supplies like food, fuel and medicine.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk

item.title: Kushner and Witkoff Traveling to Pakistan to Resume Iran Talks
item.description: Iran’s foreign minister has already arrived in the country, state media reported. He was believed to be carrying a written response to a U.S. proposal to end the war.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater, Farnaz Fassihi and Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tehran on Thursday. Iran’s foreign minister was expected to meet with Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner in Pakistan this weekend.

item.title: U.S. Unveils More Sanctions Targeting Iran’s Shadow Fleet
item.description: The Treasury Department also targeted a top independent Chinese refinery that it said was a major buyer of Iranian petroleum.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Fatemeh Bahrami/Anadolu Agency, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Kharg Island, Iran’s main hub for oil exports, in 2017. The Trump administration broadened its effort to squeeze Iran’s oil industry on Friday with a range of sanctions.

item.title: Venice Biennale Bars Awards for Countries Facing Crimes Against Humanity Charges
item.description: The jury said it would “refrain from considering” countries whose leaders are facing charges of crimes against humanity, which would affect Israel and Russia.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:description: In announcing its decision to not give awards to artists from countries facing charges of crimes against humanity, the jury of the Venice Biennale said it was committed to “the defense of human rights.”

item.title: A Disputed Jet Deal and Pressure From the Trump Administration Shake Peru’s Government
item.description: Pressure from Washington helped drive a disputed deal forward, triggering resignations and raising questions about sovereignty.
item.dc:creator: Mitra Taj and Genevieve Glatsky
item.media:description: An F-16 fighter jet during an exhibition on Thursday over Las Palmas air base in Lima, Peru.

item.title: A New Idea to Save the AMOC? Dam the Bering Strait.
item.description: Blocking the narrow waterway between Russia and Alaska could help stabilize a vulnerable system of ocean currents, scientists found in a study.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:description: A satellite view of the Bering Strait, which separates northeastern Russia and Alaska, in summer.

item.title: Israel-Lebanon Cease-Fire Tested by Escalating Clashes
item.description: The U.S.-mediated cease-fire halted an all-out war between Israel and Hezbollah, but their intensifying attacks on each other could put the truce at risk.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward and Sarah Chaayto
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Diego Ibarra Sanchez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A disabled ambulance sat amid rubble from an Israeli strike on Nabatieh, Lebanon, earlier this week. Strikes by both Israel and Hezbollah diminished since a cease-fire began last week, but clashes have intensified in recent days.

item.title: Iran and U.S. Blockades in the Strait of Hormuz: What to Know
item.description: The foes are trying to exert control over the narrow passageway. Most ships aren’t moving. Here’s what to know.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels in the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday in Musandam, Oman.

item.title: Polymarket Bets on Paris Temperature Prompt Investigation After Unusual Spikes
item.description: The authorities said a Paris airport weather sensor may have been tampered with as large wagers were placed on the betting site.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe and Ana Castelain
item.media:description: Paris Charles de Gaulle Airport, the main airport in Paris.

item.title: Trump Extends Waiver Allowing Foreign Ships to Move Goods Between U.S. Ports
item.description: The move was intended to ensure the flow of fuel in the United States, but some economists say it might reduce gas prices for consumers only a small amount.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.media:description: A Portuguese tanker docked in Braintree, Mass., last month, after President Trump waived the Jones Act to allow foreign-flagged vessels to transport goods between American ports.

item.title: Why Everyone, Including Trump, Is Talking About Airline Mergers
item.description: A few carriers are making a lot of money while many are struggling. President Trump and industry executives seem to think mergers may be the answer.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Dakota Santiago for The New York Times
item.media:description: After years of mounting costs, thin profits and a greater emphasis on selling premium seats, the airline industry is buzzing about possible mergers, partnerships and a government bailout.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater, Helene Cooper, John Ismay, Farnaz Fassihi and Max Bearak

item.title: Iran War Has Drained U.S. Supplies of Critical, Costly Weapons
item.description: The Pentagon’s rush to rearm its Mideast forces makes it less ready to confront potential adversaries like Russia and China, administration and congressional officials say.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt and Jonathan Swan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: The remains of a university building in Tehran. Two independent groups say the U.S. expense of the war in Iran so far is between $28 billion and $35 billion.

item.title: Soldier Used Classified Information to Bet on Maduro’s Ouster, U.S. Says
item.description: Federal prosecutors say that Sgt. Gannon Ken Van Dyke, who was involved in the operation to oust Nicolás Maduro from power in Venezuela, used the information to place bets on a prediction market.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Weiser and Jonah E. Bromwich
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Nicolás Maduro, former President of Venezuela, being escorted off a helicopter to be taken to the federal courthouse in Manhattan in January.

item.title: Carney Snaps Back at Trump Officials as Canada and U.S. Squabble Over Trade Deal
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney shot back at Trump administration officials, saying Canada is not ‘taking instructions from the United States.’
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen and Ana Swanson
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada at a housing announcement in suburban Ottawa on Thursday.

item.title: Trump Keeps Talking About Iran’s ‘Nuclear Dust.’ What Is It?
item.description: First of all, it isn’t dust. It’s Iran’s stockpile of near-bomb-grade uranium, which is stored in large canisters about the size of scuba tanks.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A satellite image from February showing construction on buildings at the Isfahan nuclear site in Iran that were destroyed during U.S. airstrikes last June.

item.title: The Generals Running Iran
item.description: The war has empowered the Revolutionary Guards and seems to be shifting the theocratic republic toward something more like a military regime.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times

item.title: Rescue Effort for Russian Tanker Fails, in a New Era of Maritime Peril
item.description: The ship, which carried fuel and natural gas, has been adrift for weeks in the Mediterranean Sea after a drone attack, alarming officials who fear an environmental disaster.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Miguela Xuereb/Newsbook Malta, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The Arctic Metagaz, a Russian tanker, was adrift between Malta and Lampedusa, Italy, last month.

item.title: Lebanese Journalist Killed in Israeli Strike Was Known for Being Intrepid
item.description: Mourners paid respects to Amal Khalil, who remained trapped under rubble for hours before emergency medics recovered her body.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak and Daniel Berehulak
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Family members of Amal Khalil at her funeral in Baysariyeh, Lebanon, on Thursday.

item.title: How the Supreme Court’s Shadow Docket Upended Climate Policy
item.description: The Times unearthed memos that signaled a major shift in the court’s operations, in a decision that critics say was rushed and flawed.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court has used a “shadow docket” to grant President Trump more than 20 victories on issues like immigration and employee firings.

item.title: Tanzania Report Blames ‘Outside Forces’ for Deadly Election Violence
item.description: The findings in a new report by a commission appointed by the government were at odds with details from human rights groups, which estimate that hundreds and possibly thousands of people were killed.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Protesters trying to barricade a road during clashes in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania, in October, during presidential elections.

item.title: Crash That Killed 2 Americans Is a Reminder of the Depth of Security Ties in Mexico
item.description: The deaths of two C.I.A. officers in a crash in northern Mexico sparked questions about authorization and sovereignty, but also reflected a longstanding, sensitive security partnership between the two countries.
item.dc:creator: Paulina Villegas
item.category: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives
item.media:description: A suspected methamphetamine processing lab in the northern Mexican state of Chihuahua, the state where two C.I.A. officers died in a car crash this month.

item.title: Rodrigo Duterte Will Stand Trial for Crimes Against Humanity, Court Rules
item.description: The International Criminal Court found there was enough evidence to try Rodrigo Duterte, the former Philippine president, for killings carried out during his crackdown on drugs.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Daniel Berehulak for The New York Times
item.media:description: Crime scene investigators at the scene of a killing in Manila in October 2016, months after Rodrigo Duterte became president of the Philippines.

item.title: Afghans Who Helped U.S. Forces Say They’re Being Pushed Back to the Taliban
item.description: Once promised a move to the United States, Afghan refugees who helped U.S. forces say they face ‘bad or worse’ options: resettlement to Congo or returning home to live under the Taliban.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier
item.media:credit: Sgt. Jimmie Baker/U.S. Army, via Getty Images
item.media:description: A photograph released by the U.S. Army showing Afghans at Camp As Sayliyah, in Qatar, in August 2021.

item.title: Journalist Detained in Kuwait After Posting About Iran War Is Acquitted
item.description: Ahmed Shihab-Eldin, a Kuwaiti-American journalist, is expected to be released soon, after 52 days in detention, a lawyer for two of his family members said.
item.dc:creator: Ismaeel Naar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Ahmed Shihab‑Eldin at a film festival in Doha, Qatar, last year.

item.title: What Questions Do You Have About the Trump-Xi Summit?
item.description: “Ask a Correspondent” will take your questions to David Pierson, who covers China’s foreign policy for The Times.

item.title: World’s Top Producer of Condoms Raises Prices as Iran War Rattles Supply Chains
item.description: The Malaysian company Karex, which produces about five billion condoms a year, said it was raising prices by 30 percent because of higher raw material prices and global shipping disruptions.
item.dc:creator: Zunaira Saieed
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Karex, a condom maker, said the conflict in Iran is causing an increase in production costs.

item.title: Russian Officials Say Town’s Air Is Toxic, Days After Strike on Oil Refinery
item.description: Since Ukraine attacked the refinery in Tuapse, starting a huge fire, residents have reported drops of dark, oily toxins falling from the sky.
item.dc:creator: Nataliya Vasilyeva
item.media:description: A satellite image released by Planet Labs on Tuesday showed smoke rising from an oil refinery in Tuapse, a Black Sea resort town in Russia, where a Ukrainian drone strike started a fire.

item.title: Zelensky details deals Ukraine signed to bolster Gulf nations’ defense against Iranian drones.
item.dc:creator: Maria Varenikova
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine said that Ukraine had signed agreements to cooperate with Saudi Arabia, Qatar and the United Arab Emirates on technology to intercept attack drones.

item.title: How People in Lebanon Are Reacting to Talks With Israel
item.description: The Lebanese are deeply divided over whether rare direct talks with Israel are a good thing.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A funeral in southern Lebanon on Wednesday for people killed during the war between Hezbollah and Israel.

item.title: U.S. Forces Board a 2nd Tanker They Say Is Carrying Oil From Iran
item.description: It was the second such action this week, as the Trump administration ramps up pressure on Tehran.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A photo released by the Defense Department shows the M/T Majestic X. The Pentagon said U.S. forces boarded the vessel because it was carrying Iranian oil.

item.title: Germany, a Steadfast Ally of Israel, Now Voices Some Frustration
item.description: In recent weeks, German officials have issued critical comments about Israel that convey Berlin’s recent frustration with its longtime ally.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:description: Chancellor Friedrich Merz of Germany, as well as cabinet minsters and government press officials, has begun using pointed language in discussions of Israel and its actions in the Middle East.

item.title: Will Bulgaria’s New Leader Cast His Lot With Europe or Russia?
item.description: With a record of pro-Russia statements, Rumen Radev handily won elections this week. Now Bulgarians and his E.U. partners wait to see which way he will turn.
item.dc:creator: Carlotta Gall
item.media:description: Rumen Radev campaigning in Sofia, Bulgaria, last week.

item.title: As U.S. Brands Stumble, China Wins Over Young Indonesians
item.description: A new generation of Indonesian consumers view Chinese brands as high-tech and of good quality, a departure from the days when “made in China” was associated with cheap goods.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Hasya Nindita
item.media:credit: Ulet Ifansasti for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Glodok market in the Chinatown area of Jakarta, Indonesia’s capital, decked out for Lunar New Year in February.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater, Euan Ward, Max Bearak and Chris Cameron

item.title: Trains Collide in Denmark, Leaving 5 Critically Injured
item.description: The head-on collision of two local trains happened north of Copenhagen on Thursday morning, the authorities said. The cause was not yet known.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:credit: Steven Knap/Ritzau Scanpix, via Agence France-Presse
item.media:description: The scene of the crash on Thursday, north of the town of Hillerod. Seventeen people were hospitalized, the Danish authorities said.

item.title: War in Iran Gives New Fuel to a Tax Debate in Australia
item.description: The world’s third-largest exporter of natural gas, Australia has been too lenient in taxing lucrative gas exports, many in the country argue.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Kim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Matthew Abbott for The New York Times
item.media:description: A liquefied natural gas plant on the Burrup Peninsula in Western Australia.

item.title: A Lost Tribe in India Makes Its Exodus to Israel
item.description: The B’nei Menashe believe they are the descendants of Manasseh, a tribal patriarch from thousands of years ago. Evidence is thin, but Israel is welcoming them “home.”
item.dc:creator: Hari Kumar, Alex Travelli and Atul Loke
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: People from B’nei Menashe community pray at a synagogue in the kibbutz near Churachandpur, in India’s Manipur state, in March.

item.title: Israeli Strikes Kill a Journalist and Injure Another in Lebanon
item.description: Lebanese officials say rescue teams came under fire while trying to aid Amal Khalil, a reporter for a Lebanese newspaper, and Zeinab Faraj, a photojournalist.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Ahn
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Amal Khalil, who worked for Al-Akhbar, reporting near a destroyed bridge last month. She was killed on Wednesday.

item.title: King Charles Will Not Meet Epstein Victims During U.S. State Visit
item.description: Representative Ro Khanna had asked Buckingham Palace for a private meeting between the victims and King Charles during his planned visit to the U.S. next week.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: Lawyers representing King Charles said he will not meet with victims of Jeffrey Epstein’s abuse when the monarch visits the U.S. next week.

item.title: Iran Says It Seized Two Ships Near the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: A British naval agency said the two vessels had come under attack near the strait, as a U.S. blockade on Iran’s ports continued.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel, Leo Sands and Michael Levenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A poster of the new supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, in Tehran on Tuesday.
